summary: Every triangle's interior angles sum to 180°, and an exterior angle equals the sum of the two remote interior angles.

formulas.name: Exterior angle theorem
formulas.expression: \text{exterior angle} = \text{sum of the two remote interior angles}

explanation: The three interior angles of any triangle always sum to \(180^\circ\). An exterior angle (formed by extending one side) is supplementary to its adjacent interior angle, and equals the sum of the other two ("remote") interior angles — this exterior angle theorem is often faster than working through \(180^\circ\) twice. Triangles are classified by sides (equilateral: all sides equal; isosceles: two sides equal; scalene: no sides equal) and by angles (acute, right, obtuse). In an isosceles triangle, the angles opposite the two equal sides are also equal. A triangle's area is half its base times its height, where the height is the perpendicular distance from the base to the opposite vertex — not necessarily one of the triangle's own sides.
